The right side of the T10 region injury results in loss of pain and temperature sensations on the left side, as these sensations are transmitted via the spinothalamic tract, which crosses over to the opposite side of the spinal cord.

Agent_Evidence.reasoning: In Brown-Sequard syndrome, a hemisection of the spinal cord leads to a characteristic dissociation of sensory and motor functions. The right-sided tumor at the T10 level would disrupt the corticospinal tract and dorsal columns on the right side, affecting motor function and proprioception on the same side (right leg). Conversely, the spinothalamic tract, which carries pain and temperature sensations, crosses over to the opposite side (left side) at the level of the spinal cord and ascends to the brain. Therefore, the patient would lose pain and temperature sensations in the left leg due to the contralateral effects of the injury.
cited_evidence: Loss of pain and temperature sensation on the contralateral side below the lesion in Brown-Sequard syndrome
missing_evidence: Specific sensory testing results for the left leg
safety_concerns: Potential for mismanagement of sensory deficits leading to falls or injuries

Agent_Treatment.reasoning: In Brown-Sequard syndrome, there is a characteristic pattern of sensory loss due to the hemisection of the spinal cord.
